title: Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Social Studies
culturalContext: Students will explore kaitiakitanga through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, a significant local waterway that holds deep cultural, historical and ecological importance to Ngāti Kahungunu. The stream's name references the native tree ferns that once dominated its banks and its role as a traditional food gathering site and boundary marker between hapū.
learningObjectives: Analyze historical and contemporary human impacts on Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using primary source evidence
Explain the concept of kaitiakitanga through Ngāti Kahungunu perspectives and practices related to waterway management
Collaboratively develop and present a kaitiakitanga action plan addressing a specific challenge facing Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku
Evaluate different approaches to environmental guardianship using mātauranga Māori and scientific frameworks

activities.title: Whakapapa o Te Awa: Stream Story Mapping
activities.description: Students create layered digital maps using ArcGIS showing Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku's historical course, significant sites (pā, kāinga, mahinga kai), current land use, and ecological data. They incorporate oral histories from local kaumatua about the stream's traditional significance.

activities.title: Wai Māori, Wai Ora: Water Quality Investigation
activities.description: Working with local environmental scientists, students conduct water testing at three points along the stream using both scientific methods (pH, nitrate levels, macroinvertebrate surveys) and mātauranga Māori indicators (water clarity, presence of native species). They compare historical and contemporary data to identify changes.

activities.title: Kōrero with Kaitiaki: Community Expert Dialogues
activities.description: Students prepare and conduct interviews with local kaitiaki including Ngāti Kahungunu representatives, regional council staff, and farmers from the catchment area. They explore different perspectives on stream management and responsibility using structured questioning protocols.

activities.title: He Rautaki Kaitiaki: Guardianship Strategy Development
activities.description: In small groups, students develop specific kaitiakitanga action plans addressing identified stream issues (e.g., riparian planting, sediment control, cultural monitoring). Plans must integrate mātauranga Māori principles with practical solutions and include implementation timelines.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated environmental management plans
Hawke's Bay Regional Council 'State of the Environment' monitoring data for Mangakōtukutuku Stream
Local experts: Hemi Coates (Ngāti Kahungunu environmental lead) and Dr. Sarah Jenkins (Hawke's Bay Regional Council freshwater scientist)
ArcGIS Online platform with historical maps and aerial photography layers

tasks: Written report analyzing human impacts on Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku using collected data
Oral presentation of kaitiakitanga action plan to panel including local iwi representatives
Reflective journal documenting learning journey and personal connection to kaitiakitanga

nzcAlignment: Social Studies L4: Understand how people participate individually and collectively in response to community challenges
Social Studies L5: Understand how people's management of resources impacts on environmental and social sustainability
Social Studies L4: Understand how the ideas and actions of people in the past have had a significant impact on people's lives
Social Studies L5: Understand how Treaty of Waitangi principles apply to resource management and environmental decision-making
